intro: The biggest real distinction between Selectel and Pkl is straightforward: Pkl is free and open-source, while Selectel is a commercial product (usage-based pricing). Selectel sits at usage-based pricing and carries moderate lock-in, while Pkl sits at free and open-source and carries low lock-in. Selectel is a fit when you need russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes — typical scenarios include russian cloud iaas with dedicated servers and object storage. Pkl is aimed at high-traffic websites and media-heavy apps, with workloads like automated build/test/deploy pipelines as the common case. The honest trade-off: Selectel is explicitly not for international companies or those with sanctions compliance requirements; Pkl is not intended for apps serving a single geographic region. On cost specifically, pricing model matters more than sticker price — one workload can make Selectel cheaper while a different shape flips the answer to Pkl. Before committing, model your actual usage against both pricing pages: seat counts, data volumes, and whether the free tier covers real workloads tend to dominate the total bill.
quick_take: Selectel is for russian companies needing local iaas, object; Pkl is for high-traffic websites and media-heavy apps; decide based on cost model and budget.
verdict_a: Choose Selectel if your primary workload is russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes.
verdict_b: Choose Pkl if your primary workload is high-traffic websites and media-heavy apps and you want a free/OSS tool with no vendor bill.

faq.a: No — Pkl is free and open-source, while Selectel uses usage-based pricing. Pkl wins on sticker price, but remember that self-hosting and maintaining OSS adds engineering overhead that Selectel's paid plan removes.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Selectel to Pkl?
faq.a: Migrating from Selectel to Pkl is doable but not automatic — Selectel carries moderate lock-in. Since both tools sit in the same category (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Selectel running in parallel until Pkl is verified.

On our 0–5 developer experience scale, Pkl scores higher (5/5 vs 3/5 for Selectel). The gap is 2 points, which is a noticeable difference in day-to-day ergonomics based on the data. Still, score your own tasks: DX is subjective and tied to stack assumptions.

faq.q: Is Pkl a good alternative to Selectel?
faq.a: Pkl is in the same category as Selectel (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Pkl is aimed at high-traffic websites and media-heavy apps, while Selectel targets russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes — that difference should drive the switch decision. Note that Pkl is explicitly not for apps serving a single geographic region — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
